content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
SUPERSEDED
Practices Commission
1c2. I (.X4)
September 1,
1993
Jeffrey G.
Green
County Counsel
Mariposa County
P.O.
Box 189
Mariposa,
CA
95338
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1—93-303
Dear Mr.
Green:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Mariposa County Supervisor Garry R.
Parker regarding his
responsibilities as a Mariposa County Supervisor and a member of
the Governing Board of the Mariposa County Water Agency under the
conflict—of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
“Act”) •1
Since your advice request does not refer to a specific
governmental decision,
we are treating your request as one for
informal assistance.
2
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.
In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
71.)
Finally, please note that the Commission’s advice is limited
to the provisions of the Political Reform Act.
(Sections
81000—
91015.)
The Commission has no jurisdiction over other laws that
may apply to your facts,
such as Section 1090.
If you wish advice
with respect to these areas of the law,
you should contact the
Attorney General’s Office.
All statutory references are to the Government Code unless
otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2
California Code of Regulations Section 18000 et seq.
All
references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the
California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Bo 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1—93—303
Page 2
QUESTION
What obligations under the Act will Supervisor Parker have
with respect to his purchase of bonds issued by the Selma Public
Financing Authority for the purpose of funding various public
projects in the state including a project by the Mariposa
Municipal Utilities District?
CONCLUSION
The Act exempts bonds and other debt instruments issued by
governments or government agencies from the definitions of both
“investment” and “income.”
Since, according to your facts, the
bonds are municipal bonds issued by a public entity, the bonds are
not disciosable under the Act.
The Act prohibits public officials from participating in
decisions that will materially affect their own assets and income.
Thus, to the extent that a decision of the supervisor will affect
the value of the bond or income received from the bond,
by $250 or
more,
he would have a conflict of interest.
FACTS
Supervisor Parker is a Mariposa County Supervisor.
The
Mariposa County Board of Supervisors also sits as the Board of
Directors for the Mariposa County Water Agency (the “agency”).
He
is contemplating purchasing some tax—free municipal bands to be
issued by the Selma Public Financing Authority for the purpose of
funding various public projects in the state including a project
by the Mariposa Municipal Utilities District (the “district”).
The district is located in Mariposa County.
The county and
the agency occasionally do business with the district.
For
example,
in the past, the agency has loaned money to the district
to do projects within the county.
You stated that none of the
loans are related to the bond issue.
You have asked whether purchasing the municipal bonds will
create a conflict of interest for the supervisor in decisions
affecting the district.
ANALYSIS
1.
Economic Interests
Section 87100 of the Act does prohibit any public official
from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using the
official’s position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
Section 87103 provides:
An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material finncial effect, distinguishable


Our File No.
1—93—303
Page 3
from its effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family
or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income,
other than gift and
other than loans by a commercial lending institu
tion in the regular course of business on terms
available to the public without regard to official
status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to, received by or
promised to the public official within 12 months
prior to the time when the decision is made.
Sections 87103(a)
and (c).
Thus,
Supervisor Parker is prohibited from participating in
any decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on his economic interests.
According to your facts, Supervisor Parker wishes to purchase
bonds from the financing authority which will use the funds for a
project in the district.
If these bonds’constituted an
“investment” in a “business entity,” he would have an economic
interest in decisions that affect the financing authority or the
district.
However, Section 82034 defines “investment” as any financial
interest in or security issued by a “business entity,” but
excludes any bond or debt instrument issued by a government
agency.
Moreover,
Section 82005 defines a “business entity” as
any organization or enterprise operated for profit, including but
not limited to a proprietorship, partnership,
firm, business
trust, joint venture,
syndicate, corporation or association.
This
definition would not include governmental agencies.
(Fatland
Advice Letter,
No.
1-89-419.)
Thus,
Supervisor Parker would not
have an interest in the district by virtue of the bonds under
Section 87103(a).
Additionally, the definition of “income” in Section 82030
excludes: “interest, dividends,
or premiums on a time or demand
deposit in a financial institution,
shares in a credit union or
any insurance policy,
payments received under any insurance
policy,
or any bond or other debt instrument issued by any
government or government agency.”
(Section 82030(b) (4).)
Consequently,
Supervisor Parker would not have an interest in
the district by virtue of interest received from the bonds.


Our File No.
1—93—303
Page
4
Moreover, the supervisor would not be required to disqualify
himself, pursuant to Section 87103(a)
and (c),
from a decision
that affects the district.
B.
Direct Effects on the Official or His Immediate Family
Section 87103 and Regulation 18702.1(a) (4) provide that
decisions which will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on the official’s personal expenses,
income,
assets, or liabilities are also potentially disqualifying
financial interests.
Consequently, Supervisor Parker may not make, participate in
making or in any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the
value of the bonds or the interest he receives.
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reason
ably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.
An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)
Thus,
for example, if it is substantially likely that a
decision of the board of supervisors will materially affect the
value of the bonds,
or their repayment, the supervisor would be
prohibited from participating in the decision.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—590l.
Sincerely,
Wayne Ordos
cecutive Director
By:
John W. Wallace
Counsel,
Legal Division
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively,
copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of l0c per page.

QUESTION: What obligations under the Act will Supervisor Parker have
with respect to his purchase of bonds issued by the Selma Public
Financing Authority for the purpose of funding various public
projects in the state including a project by the Mariposa
Municipal Utilities District?

CONCLUSION: The Act exempts bonds and other debt instruments issued by
governments or government agencies from the definitions of both
“investment” and “income.”
Since, according to your facts, the
bonds are municipal bonds issued by a public entity, the bonds are
not disciosable under the Act.
The Act prohibits public officials from participating in
decisions that will materially affect their own assets and income.
Thus, to the extent that a decision of the supervisor will affect
the value of the bond or income received from the bond,
by $250 or
more,
he would have a conflict of interest.
